g.data-narration: Welcome to interrupts and interrupt handling. We will explore how CPUs respond to urgent events efficiently.
Without interrupts, CPUs must poll devices continuously, wasting cycles. Interrupts let devices alert the CPU only when needed.
An interrupt is a signal requiring immediate attention. The CPU suspends current work, saves state, and runs a handler routine.
Hardware interrupts come from external devices like keyboards and network cards. They are asynchronous to program execution.
Software interrupts are triggered by programs using special instructions like INT n. They request OS services like file operations.
Exceptions are generated by the CPU when encountering errors like division by zero or page faults. They are synchronous to execution.
Hardware interrupts are async from devices. Software interrupts are sync from programs. Exceptions are sync CPU-generated errors.
The interrupt handling process has six steps: occurrence, CPU acknowledgement, state saving, finding the handler, executing it, and resuming.
The CPU saves the Program Counter and Processor Status Word onto the stack to resume the interrupted program exactly where it left off.
The Interrupt Vector Table maps interrupt numbers to handler addresses. The CPU uses the interrupt vector to find which ISR to execute.
The ISR saves additional registers, services the device, sends End of Interrupt signal, restores registers, and executes RTI.
The RTI instruction pops saved PC and PSW from the stack. The CPU resumes the interrupted program from exactly where it stopped.
g.text: From exact point - transparent to user

g.data-narration: Interrupt masking disables interrupts to protect critical code. Priority handles simultaneous interrupts. DMA reduces interrupt overhead by bulk transfers.
DMA allows devices to transfer data directly to memory. The CPU initiates the transfer, then continues work while DMA handles the data movement.
In summary, interrupts enable efficient I/O by allowing devices to alert the CPU. Types include hardware, software, and exceptions with distinct characteristics.
g.text: ✓ Interrupts = Efficient CPU notification vs polling
✓ Three types: Hardware (async), Software/Trap (sync), Exceptions (CPU errors)
✓ 6-step process: Occur → Acknowledge → Save State → Get Vector → Execute ISR → RTI
✓ IVT maps vectors to ISR addresses for quick routing

g.data-narration: For exams, memorize the interrupt handling sequence, understand IVT purpose, and know how DMA reduces interrupt overhead in I/O operations.
You can now define interrupts, classify their types, describe the handling sequence, explain IVT, differentiate masking types, and analyze DMA interaction.
Thank you for studying interrupts and interrupt handling. This fundamental concept is essential for understanding computer system architecture.
